question_html: There are two coils A and B. A current starts flowing in B if the coil A is made to rotate about a vertical axis (Figure). No current flows in B if A is at rest. The current in coil A, when the current in B (at t = 0) is counterclockwise and the coil A is as shown at this instant, t = 0, is

options.explanation: This problem is solved by using Lenz's law. At t = 0 current in B is counter-clockwise and coil A is considered above B. The counter-clockwise flow of the current in B is equivalent to the north pole of the magnet and magnetic field lines are emanating upward to coil A. When coil A start rotating at t = 0, the current in A is constant along clockwise direction by Lenz's rule. As for flux changes across coil A by rotating it near the N-pole formed by flowing current in B in anticlockwise.

solution_text: (a), (b) and (d) are correct. A plate or conductor is heated by the heating effect of current (H = IRt). Verify answers (a) (d). When a time-dependent magnetic field varies across the metal plate due to the production of eddy currents, heating of plate takes place verify (b).

solution_text: (a), (b) and (c) are correct. E.m.f. produced in a coil due to change in magnetic flux with time in option (a), (b) and (c). question_text: The mutual inductance M 12 of coil 1 with respect to coil 2 increases when they are brought nearer. depends on the current passing through the coils. increases when one of them is rotated about an axis. is the same as M 21 of coil 2 with respect to coil 1.
question_html:
The mutual inductance M12 of coil 1 with respect to coil 2

  1. increases when they are brought nearer.
  2. depends on the current passing through the coils.
  3. increases when one of them is rotated about an axis.
  4. is the same as M21 of coil 2 with respect to coil 1.
solution_text: (a) and (d) are correct. Mutual Induction: Whenever the current passing through a coil or circuit changes, the magnetic flux linked with a neighbouring coil or circuit will also change. Hence an emf will be induced in the neighbouring coil or circuit. This phenomenon is called ‘mutual induction’. As we know that M 12 = M 21 = {tex}\mu_{0} n_{1} n_{2} \pi r_{1}^{2}\over l{/tex} where r 1 {tex}l{/tex} is the common area of cross-section of coil so M 12 does not depend on passing current and rotation. {tex}l{/tex} is also common length. So answer (a) and (d) verified.
solution_html:
(a) and (d) are correct.
Mutual Induction: Whenever the current passing through a coil or circuit changes, the magnetic flux linked with a neighbouring coil or circuit will also change. Hence an emf will be induced in the neighbouring coil or circuit. This phenomenon is called ‘mutual induction’.
As we know that M12 = M21 = {tex}\mu_{0} n_{1} n_{2} \pi r_{1}^{2}\over l{/tex} where r1 {tex}l{/tex} is the common area of cross-section of coil so M12 does not depend on passing current and rotation. {tex}l{/tex} is also common length. So answer (a) and (d) verified.

question_html:
A circular coil expands radially in a region of the magnetic field and no electromotive force is produced in the coil. This can be because

  1. the magnetic field is constant.
  2. the magnetic field is in the same plane as the circular coil and it may or may not vary.
  3. the magnetic field has a perpendicular (to the plane of the coil) component whose magnitude is decreasing suitably.
  4. there is a constant magnetic field in the perpendicular (to the plane of the coil) direction.
solution_text: (b) and (c) are correct. When a circular coil expands radially in a region of the magnetic field, induced emf developed is e = Blv = B {tex}\times{/tex} rate of change of area It is given that the magnetic field B is in a plane perpendicular to the plane of the circular coil. since e = 0, the magnetic field should be in the plane of circular coil so that its component which perpendicular to plane of coil is zero. If the magnetic field has a component perpendicular to the plane of the coil,whose magnitude is decreasing so that magnetic flux linked with the coil, then {tex}e=\frac{d \phi}{d t}=0{/tex} . Hence the option b and c.

(b) and (c) are correct.
When a circular coil expands radially in a region of the magnetic field, induced emf developed is e = Blv = B {tex}\times{/tex} rate of change of area It is given that the magnetic field B is in a plane perpendicular to the plane of the circular coil. since e = 0, the magnetic field should be in the plane of circular coil so that its component which perpendicular to plane of coil is zero. If the magnetic field has a component perpendicular to the plane of the coil,whose magnitude is decreasing so that  magnetic flux linked with the coil, then {tex}e=\frac{d \phi}{d t}=0{/tex}. question_text: A wire in the form of a tightly wound solenoid is connected to a DC source and carries a current. If the coil is stretched so that there are gaps between successive elements of the spiral coil, will the current increase or decrease? Explain.
question_html: A wire in the form of a tightly wound solenoid is connected to a DC source and carries a current. If the coil is stretched so that there are gaps between successive elements of the spiral coil, will the current increase or decrease? Explain.
solution_text: The current will increase. When the coil is stretched the magnet flux with a leak through the gaps between two turns. According to Lenz’s law, the e.m.f. is produced in the nearby coil which will oppose the increase or decrease in flux in the coil. So, on stretching the coil, the current will increase. When the coil is stretched then n (no. of turns per unit length) will decrease. So L decreases or reactance decreases which makes the current increase.
solution_html: The current will increase.  When the coil is stretched the magnet flux with a leak through the gaps between two turns. According to Lenz’s law, the e.m.f. is produced in the nearby coil which will oppose the increase or decrease in flux in the coil. So, on stretching the coil, the current will increase. When the coil is stretched then n (no. of turns per unit length) will decrease. So L decreases or reactance decreases which makes the current increase.

question_text: A solenoid is connected to a battery so that a steady current flows through it. If an iron core is inserted into the solenoid, will the current increase or decrease? Explain.
question_html: A solenoid is connected to a battery so that a steady current flows through it. If an iron core is inserted into the solenoid, will the current increase or decrease? Explain.
solution_text: When the ferromagnetic iron core is inserted inside the solenoid the magnetic flux will increase. As flux increased, then by Lenz’s law, to oppose an increase in flux i.e., to decrease flux the current in the coil will decrease.
solution_html:
When the ferromagnetic iron core is inserted inside the solenoid the magnetic flux will increase.
As flux increased, then by Lenz’s law, to oppose an increase in flux i.e., to decrease flux the current in the coil will decrease.

question_html: Consider a metal ring kept (supported by a cardboard) on top of a fixed solenoid carrying a current I (see Figure). The centre of the ring coincides with the axis of the solenoid. If the current in the solenoid is switched off, what will happen to the ring?
solution_text: As the current was already flowing through the solenoid so it behaves like a magnet and let S pole is upper side a flux in-ring is constant. So there is no induced current in ring. When current is switched off, magnetic flux decrease so induced current produced in the ring in such a way so that it can increase the flux. So the North pole is produced in the ring in the lower side and attracted by a solenoid. So, downward and solenoid are fixed rings will not be able to move downward.

When current is switched off, magnetic flux decrease so induced current produced in the ring in such a way so that it can increase the flux. question_text: Consider a metallic pipe with an inner radius of 1 cm. If a cylindrical bar magnet of radius 0.8cm is dropped through the pipe, it takes more time to come down than it takes for a similar unmagnetized cylindrical iron bar dropped through the metallic pipe. Explain.
question_html: Consider a metallic pipe with an inner radius of 1 cm. If a cylindrical bar magnet of radius 0.8cm is dropped through the pipe, it takes more time to come down than it takes for a similar unmagnetized cylindrical iron bar dropped through the metallic pipe. Explain.
solution_text: When a cylindrical bar magnet of radius 0.8 cm is dropped inside a hollow cylindrical metallic pipe of radius 1 cm the magnet flux across the pipe change, and due to induction, eddy current will produce. The direction of eddy current will be in such a way so that it can oppose the cause i.e., the motion of the magnet. So upward force acts on a bar magnet and decreases the acceleration, which in turn takes more time to fall magnet through the pipe, as compared to a simple metallic piece in place of the magnet. When a similar unmagnetised cylindrical iron bar is dropped through the metallic pipe, there are no eddy currents as magnetic flux linked with the pipe does not change. Hence, there is no opposition to free fall of the iron bar under gravity, i.e, its downward acceleration = g. Hence, the iron bar takes lesser time to come down the pipe.
solution_html:
When a cylindrical bar magnet of radius 0.8 cm is dropped inside a hollow cylindrical metallic pipe of radius 1 cm the magnet flux across the pipe change, and due to induction, eddy current will produce.
The direction of eddy current will be in such a way so that it can oppose the cause i.e., the motion of the magnet. So upward force acts on a bar magnet and decreases the acceleration, which in turn takes more time to fall magnet through the pipe, as compared to a simple metallic piece in place of the magnet. When a similar unmagnetised cylindrical iron bar is dropped through the metallic pipe, there are no eddy currents as magnetic flux linked with the pipe does not change. Hence, there is no opposition to free fall of the iron bar under gravity, i.e, its downward acceleration = g. Hence, the iron bar takes lesser time to come down the pipe.

question_text: A magnetic field in a certain region is given by B = Bo cos ( {tex}\Omega{/tex} t) {tex}\hat{k}{/tex} and a coil of radius a with resistance R is placed in the x-y plane with its centre at the origin in the magnetic field (see Fig). Find the magnitude and the direction of the current at (a, 0, 0) at {tex}t=\frac{\pi}{2 \omega}, t=\frac{\pi}{\omega}{/tex} and {tex}t=\frac{3 \pi}{2 \omega}{/tex} .
question_html: A magnetic field in a certain region is given by B = Bo cos ({tex}\Omega{/tex}t) {tex}\hat{k}{/tex} and a coil of radius a with resistance R is placed in the x-y plane with its centre at the origin in the magnetic field (see Fig). Find the magnitude and the direction of the current at (a, 0, 0) at {tex}t=\frac{\pi}{2 \omega}, t=\frac{\pi}{\omega}{/tex} and {tex}t=\frac{3 \pi}{2 \omega}{/tex}.
solution_text: The direction of magnetic field is along Z-axis. So the magnetic flux passing through circular region of coil of radius a placed in X-Y plane. {tex}\phi=\vec{B} \cdot \vec{A}=B A \cos \theta{/tex} The direction area vector A of coil is along Z-direction and B also in Z-direction of {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0 o {tex}\phi=\left(B_{0} \cos \omega t\right) \cdot\left(\pi a^{2}\right) \cos 0^{\circ}{/tex} {tex}\phi=B_{0} \pi a^{2} \cos \omega t{/tex} By Faraday’s law of electromagnetic induction, {tex}\varepsilon=\frac{d \phi}{d t} = \frac d{dt}(B_{0} \pi a^{2} cos \omega t ){/tex} {tex}\varepsilon=B_{0} \pi a^{2} \omega \sin \omega t{/tex} Flow of induced current in coil {tex}I=\frac{\varepsilon}{R}{/tex} where R is resistance of coil. {tex}I=\frac{B_{0} \pi a^{2} \omega}{R} \sin \omega t{/tex} Current at {tex}t=\frac{\pi}{2 \omega}{/tex} , {tex}I = \frac{{{B_0}\pi {a^2}\omega }}{R}\sin \omega \cdot \frac{\pi }{{2\omega }} = \frac{{{B_0}\pi {a^2}\omega }}{R}\sin \frac{\pi }{2} {/tex} = {tex}\frac{B_{0} \pi a^{2} \omega}{R}{/tex} (along {tex}\hat{j}{/tex} ) Current at {tex}t=\frac{\pi}{ \omega}{/tex} , {tex}I=\frac{B_{0} \pi a^{2} \omega}{R} \sin \omega \frac{\pi}{\omega}{/tex} {tex}I=\frac{B_{0} \pi a^{2} \omega}{R} \sin \pi=\frac{B_{0} \pi a^{2} \omega}{R} \times 0=0{/tex} Current at {tex}t=\frac{3 \pi}{2 \omega}{/tex} , {tex}I=\frac{B_{0} \pi a^{2} \omega}{R} \sin \omega \cdot \frac{3 \pi}{\omega}{/tex} {tex}I=\frac{B_{0} \pi a^{2} \omega}{R} \sin 3 \pi{/tex} {tex}=\frac{B_{0} \pi a^{2} \omega}{R} \sin \pi{/tex} {tex}I=\frac{B_{0} \pi a^{2} \omega}{R}{/tex} (along - {tex}\hat{j}{/tex} )
solution_html:
The direction of magnetic field is along Z-axis. So the magnetic flux passing through circular region of coil of radius a placed in X-Y plane.
{tex}\phi=\vec{B} \cdot \vec{A}=B A \cos \theta{/tex}
The direction area vector A of coil is along Z-direction and B also in Z-direction of {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0o
{tex}\phi=\left(B_{0} \cos \omega t\right) \cdot\left(\pi a^{2}\right) \cos 0^{\circ}{/tex} 
{tex}\phi=B_{0} \pi a^{2} \cos \omega t{/tex} 
By Faraday’s law of electromagnetic induction,
{tex}\varepsilon=\frac{d \phi}{d t} = \frac d{dt}(B_{0} \pi a^{2} cos \omega t ){/tex} 
{tex}\varepsilon=B_{0} \pi a^{2} \omega \sin \omega t{/tex} 
Flow of induced current in coil {tex}I=\frac{\varepsilon}{R}{/tex} where R is resistance of coil.
{tex}I=\frac{B_{0} \pi a^{2} \omega}{R} \sin \omega t{/tex} 
Current at {tex}t=\frac{\pi}{2 \omega}{/tex},
{tex}I = \frac{{{B_0}\pi {a^2}\omega }}{R}\sin \omega \cdot \frac{\pi }{{2\omega }} = \frac{{{B_0}\pi {a^2}\omega }}{R}\sin \frac{\pi }{2} {/tex} = {tex}\frac{B_{0} \pi a^{2} \omega}{R}{/tex} (along {tex}\hat{j}{/tex})
Current at {tex}t=\frac{\pi}{ \omega}{/tex},
{tex}I=\frac{B_{0} \pi a^{2} \omega}{R} \sin \omega \frac{\pi}{\omega}{/tex} 
{tex}I=\frac{B_{0} \pi a^{2} \omega}{R} \sin \pi=\frac{B_{0} \pi a^{2} \omega}{R} \times 0=0{/tex} 
Current at {tex}t=\frac{3 \pi}{2 \omega}{/tex} , 
{tex}I=\frac{B_{0} \pi a^{2} \omega}{R} \sin \omega \cdot \frac{3 \pi}{\omega}{/tex} 
{tex}I=\frac{B_{0} \pi a^{2} \omega}{R} \sin 3 \pi{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{B_{0} \pi a^{2} \omega}{R} \sin \pi{/tex} 
{tex}I=\frac{B_{0} \pi a^{2} \omega}{R}{/tex} (along -{tex}\hat{j}{/tex})

question_text: Consider a closed loop C in a magnetic field (Figure). The flux passing through the loop is defined by choosing a surface whose edge coincides with the loop and using the formula {tex}\phi=\mathbf{B}_{1} \cdot \boldsymbol{d} \mathbf{A}_{1}+\boldsymbol{B}_{2} \cdot \boldsymbol{d} \mathbf{A}_{2}+\ldots{/tex} Now if we chose two different surfaces S 1 and S 2 having C as their edge, would we get the same answer for flux. Justify your answer.
question_html:
Consider a closed loop C in a magnetic field (Figure). The flux passing through the loop is defined by choosing a surface whose edge coincides with the loop and using the formula {tex}\phi=\mathbf{B}_{1} \cdot \boldsymbol{d} \mathbf{A}_{1}+\boldsymbol{B}_{2} \cdot \boldsymbol{d} \mathbf{A}_{2}+\ldots{/tex}
Now if we chose two different surfaces S1 and S2 having C as their edge, would we get the same answer for flux. Justify your answer.
solution_text: The magnetic flux lines passing through can be considered as the number of magnetic field lines passing through the surface. So let d {tex}\phi=\vec{B}{/tex} . A represents magnetic field lines in an area A to magnetic flux B. By the concept of continuity of line B cannot end or start in space, therefore, the number of lines passing through S 1 must be the same as the number of lines passing through S 2 surface. Therefore, in both cases, we get the same flux.

question_text: Find the current in the wire for the configuration shown in Figure. Wire PQ has negligible resistance. B, the magnetic field is coming out of the paper. {tex}\theta{/tex} is a fixed angle made by PQ travelling smoothly over two conducting parallel wires separated by a distance d?
question_html: Find the current in the wire for the configuration shown in Figure. Wire PQ has negligible resistance. B, the magnetic field is coming out of the paper. {tex}\theta{/tex} is a fixed angle made by PQ travelling smoothly over two conducting parallel wires separated by a distance d?
solution_text: The motional electric field E due to the motion along CD is E = vB. PQ is a wire of negligible resistance travelling smoothly over two conducting parallel wires X and Y separated by distance (d) The direction of E will be {tex}\perp{/tex} to both v and B ( {tex}\because{/tex} F = q {tex}\times B{/tex} ) F is force on free charge particle of PQ. So the motional e.m.f. {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = E along PQ {tex}\times{/tex} effective length PQ Electric field along PQ = {tex}v{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} B = {tex}v{/tex} {tex}\sin \theta B=v B \sin \theta{/tex} {tex}\varepsilon=(v B \sin \theta)\times\left(\frac{d}{\sin \theta}\right){/tex} {tex}\epsilon=v B d{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Induced current {tex}I=\frac{\varepsilon}{R}=\frac{v B d}{R}{/tex}
solution_html:
The motional electric field E due to the motion along CD is E = vB. PQ is a wire of negligible resistance travelling smoothly over two conducting parallel wires X and Y separated by distance (d)

The direction of E will be {tex}\perp{/tex} to both v and B ({tex}\because{/tex} F = q {tex}\times B{/tex})
F is force on free charge particle of PQ.
So the motional e.m.f. {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = E along PQ {tex}\times{/tex} effective length PQ
Electric field along PQ ={tex}v{/tex}{tex}\times{/tex} B = {tex}v{/tex} {tex}\sin \theta B=v B \sin \theta{/tex}
{tex}\varepsilon=(v B \sin \theta)\times\left(\frac{d}{\sin \theta}\right){/tex}
{tex}\epsilon=v B d{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Induced current {tex}I=\frac{\varepsilon}{R}=\frac{v B d}{R}{/tex}

question_text: A (current vs time) graph of the current passing through a solenoid is shown in Figure. For which time is the back electromotive force (u) a maximum. If the back emf at t = 3s is e, find the back emf at t = 7 s, 15s, and 40s. OA, AB, and BC are straight line segments.
question_html: A (current vs time) graph of the current passing through a solenoid is shown in Figure. For which time is the back electromotive force (u) a maximum. If the back emf at t = 3s is e, find the back emf at t = 7 s, 15s, and 40s. OA, AB, and BC are straight line segments.
solution_text: The maximum back electromotive force (u) will be maximum when there is a maximum rate of change of magnetic flux which is directly proportional to the rate of change of current. Maximum change or rate of current will be where (t - I) graph for the solenoid makes a maximum angle with time axis which is in part AB So the maximum back e.m.f. will occur between 5 s to 10 s. As the back e.m.f. at t = 3 s it is e (given) Rate of change of current at t = 3 s-slope of OA graph with time axis So the rate of change of current at 3s = {tex}\frac{1}{5} A / s{/tex} So back electromotive force at t = 3s {tex}=L \times \frac{1}{5}=\frac{L}{5}=e{/tex} (given) {tex}\because e=L \cdot \frac{d I}{d t}{/tex} and L = constant for the solenoid. Similarly back e.m.f. u between 5 to 10 sec. {tex}u_{1}=L\left(\frac{-3}{5}\right)=-3 \frac{L}{5}=-3 e{/tex} back e.m.f. between 10 to 30 sec {tex}u_{2}=L \frac{[0-(-2)]}{(30-10)}=\frac{+2 L}{20}=\frac{+1}{2} \frac{L}{5}{/tex} {tex}u_{2}=+\frac{1}{2} e{/tex} So back e.m.f. at 7 sec = -3 e Back e.m.f. at 15 sec {tex}=+\frac{1}{2} e{/tex} At 40 sec graph is along the time axis, i.e. its slope with time axis is zero. So, {tex}\frac{d I}{d t}=0{/tex} Or back e.m.f. at 40 sec = 0
solution_html:
The maximum back electromotive force (u) will be maximum when there is a maximum rate of change of magnetic flux which is directly proportional to the rate of change of current.
Maximum change or rate of current will be where (t - I) graph for the solenoid  makes a maximum angle with time axis which is in part AB
So the maximum back e.m.f. will occur between 5 s to 10 s. As the back e.m.f. at t = 3 s it is e (given)
Rate of change of current at t = 3 s-slope of OA graph with time axis So the rate of change of current at 3s = {tex}\frac{1}{5} A / s{/tex}
So back electromotive force at t = 3s {tex}=L \times \frac{1}{5}=\frac{L}{5}=e{/tex} (given)
{tex}\because e=L \cdot \frac{d I}{d t}{/tex} and L = constant for the solenoid.
Similarly back e.m.f. u between 5 to 10 sec.
{tex}u_{1}=L\left(\frac{-3}{5}\right)=-3 \frac{L}{5}=-3 e{/tex}
back e.m.f. between 10 to 30 sec
{tex}u_{2}=L \frac{[0-(-2)]}{(30-10)}=\frac{+2 L}{20}=\frac{+1}{2} \frac{L}{5}{/tex}
{tex}u_{2}=+\frac{1}{2} e{/tex}
So back e.m.f. at 7 sec = -3 e
Back e.m.f. at 15 sec {tex}=+\frac{1}{2} e{/tex}
At 40 sec graph is along the time axis, i.e. its slope with time axis is zero.
So, {tex}\frac{d I}{d t}=0{/tex}
Or back e.m.f. at 40 sec = 0

question_text: There are two coils A and B separated by some distance. If a current of 2 A flows through A, a magnetic flux of 10 -2 Wb passes through B (no current through B). If no current passes through A and a current of 1 A passes through B, what is the flux through A?
question_html: There are two coils A and B separated by some distance. If a current of 2 A flows through A, a magnetic flux of 10-2 Wb passes through B (no current through B). If no current passes through A and a current of 1 A passes through B, what is the flux through A?
solution_text: Let I A current is passing through coil A having mutual inductance M AB with respect to coil B. N A = number of turns in coil A N B = number of turns in coil B {tex}\phi_A{/tex} = flux linked with coil A due to coil B {tex}\phi_B{/tex} = flux linked with coil B due to coil A M BA = Mutual inductance of coil B with respect to coil A Then Total flux through B = M 2 {tex}\phi_2{/tex} = M BA I 1 10 -2 = M BA {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}M_{B A}=\frac{10^{-2}}{2}=5 \mathrm{m} H{/tex} Now total flux through A = M A {tex}\phi_A{/tex} =M AB I 2 [ {tex}\because{/tex} M AB =M BA ] = 5 mH {tex}\times{/tex} 1 Wb = 5mWb
solution_html:
Let IA current is passing through coil A having mutual inductance MAB with respect to coil B.
NA = number of turns in coil A
NB = number of turns in coil B
{tex}\phi_A{/tex}=  flux linked with coil A due to coil B
{tex}\phi_B{/tex} = flux linked with coil B due to coil A
MBA = Mutual inductance of coil B with respect to coil A
Then
Total flux through B = M2 {tex}\phi_2{/tex} = MBAI1
10-2 = MBA {tex}\times{/tex} 2
{tex}M_{B A}=\frac{10^{-2}}{2}=5 \mathrm{m} H{/tex}
Now total flux through A = MA {tex}\phi_A{/tex}=MABI2 [{tex}\because{/tex} MAB=MBA]
= 5 mH {tex}\times{/tex} 1 Wb = 5mWb

question_text: A magnetic field {tex}\mathbf{\vec B}=B_{o} \sin (\omega t) \hat{\mathbf{k}}{/tex} covers a large region where a wire AB slides smoothly over two parallel conductors separated by a distance d (Figure). The wires are in the x-y plane. The wire AB (of length d) has resistance R and the parallel wires have negligible resistance. If AB is moving with velocity v, what is the current in the circuit? What is the force needed to keep the wire moving at constant velocity?
question_html: A magnetic field {tex}\mathbf{\vec B}=B_{o} \sin (\omega t) \hat{\mathbf{k}}{/tex} covers a large region where a wire AB slides smoothly over two parallel conductors separated by a distance d (Figure). The wires are in the x-y plane. The wire AB (of length d) has resistance R and the parallel wires have negligible resistance. If AB is moving with velocity v, what is the current in the circuit? What is the force needed to keep the wire moving at constant velocity?
solution_text: In figure, CA and OB are long parallel conducting wires, connected by d and conductor CO. The resistance of ACOB is negligible. In Fig. we have shown a wire AB of length 1 -d, resistance R moving with a velocity v along positive direction of X-axis over two parallel wires OX and CD at y = 0 and y = d respectively. Let wire AB at t = 0 is at x = 0 i.e., on Y-axis. Now AB moves with velocity v {tex}\hat{i}{/tex} Let at any time t, the position of conductor AB is x(t) = v {tex}\hat{i}{/tex} t Motional e.m.f. across AB {tex}V_{A B}=\frac{W_{A B}}{q}=\frac{F d}{q}=\frac{q v \hat{i} \times B}{q} d{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{AB}}=\mathrm{v} \hat{i} \times \mathrm{B}_{0} \sin \omega \mathrm{t} \cdot \hat{k}{\times} \mathrm{d}{/tex} {tex}\varepsilon_{1}=B_{0} \sin \omega t v(\hat{i} \times \hat{k}) d=B_{0} d v \sin \omega t(-\hat{j}){/tex} {tex}\varepsilon_{1}=-B_{0} v d \sin \omega t(\hat{j}){/tex} (along -ve Y direction in AB) ...(i) e.m.f. due to change in magnetic field from t = 0 to at t = 1 when AB is at (x, 0) {tex}\therefore \varepsilon_{2}=\frac{-d \phi}{d t}=\frac{-d}{d t} \vec{B} \cdot \vec{A}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{-d}{d t}\left[B_{0} \sin \omega t \hat{k} \cdot(x \cdot d) \hat{k}\right]{/tex} Area vector A is along {tex}\hat{k}​​​{/tex} {tex}\therefore \varepsilon_{2}=\frac{-d}{d t}\left(B_{0} \sin \omega t x d\right){/tex} {tex}\varepsilon_{2}=-B_{0} x d . \omega \cos \omega{/tex} ...(ii) {tex}\therefore{/tex} Total magnitude of e.m.f. {tex}=-B_{0} v d \sin \omega t-B_{0} x d \omega \cos \omega t{/tex} {tex}\varepsilon=-B_{0} d[v \sin \omega t+x \omega \cos \omega t]{/tex} The direction of electric induced current by Fleming Right Hand Rule is from (A to B) i.e., in clockwise in the loop. {tex}I=\frac{\varepsilon}{R}=\frac{B_{0} d}{R}{/tex} {tex}[\mathrm{v} \sin \omega \mathrm{t}+\omega \mathrm{x} \cos \omega \mathrm{t}]{/tex} {tex}\theta{/tex} is the angle between {tex}B \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\mathrm{I}(-\mathrm{j})=90^{\circ}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{F}=\mathrm{I} \times \mathrm{Bdl}{/tex} {tex}=I(-\hat{j}) \times B(\hat{k}) d \sin \theta\left[\because \theta=90^{\circ}\right]{/tex} {tex}=-I B(\hat{j} \times \hat{k}) d \sin 90^{\circ}=-I B(\hat{i}) d{/tex} {tex}F=-\hat{i} I B d{/tex} It shows the direction of the force on AB is toward x-direction, which can be certified by Fleming's left-hand rule. {tex}\therefore|F|=I B d{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} To keep the wire in motion with constant velocity, it will be towards positive x-direction + opp. To electromagnetic force {tex}F=B \cdot d I=|B| \cdot d \frac{\varepsilon}{R}{/tex} {tex}F=\frac{B d B_{0} d}{R}[v \sin \omega t+\omega x \cos \omega t]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{B_{0} \sin \omega t B_{0} d^{2}}{R}[v \sin \omega t+\omega x \cos \omega t]{/tex} {tex}F=\frac{B_{0}^{2} d^{2} \sin \omega t}{R}[v \sin \omega t+\omega x \cos \omega t]{/tex}
solution_html:
In figure, CA and OB are long parallel conducting wires, connected by d and conductor CO. The resistance of ACOB is negligible.

In Fig. we have shown a wire AB of length 1 -d, resistance R moving with a velocity v along positive direction of X-axis over two parallel wires OX and CD at y = 0 and y = d respectively.

Let wire AB at t = 0 is at x = 0 i.e., on Y-axis. Now AB moves with velocity v {tex}\hat{i}{/tex}
Let at any time t, the position of conductor AB is x(t) = v{tex}\hat{i}{/tex}t
Motional e.m.f. across AB
{tex}V_{A B}=\frac{W_{A B}}{q}=\frac{F d}{q}=\frac{q v \hat{i} \times B}{q} d{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{AB}}=\mathrm{v} \hat{i} \times \mathrm{B}_{0} \sin \omega \mathrm{t} \cdot \hat{k}{\times} \mathrm{d}{/tex}
{tex}\varepsilon_{1}=B_{0} \sin \omega t v(\hat{i} \times \hat{k}) d=B_{0} d v \sin \omega t(-\hat{j}){/tex}
{tex}\varepsilon_{1}=-B_{0} v d \sin \omega t(\hat{j}){/tex} (along -ve Y direction in AB) ...(i)
e.m.f. due to change in magnetic field from t = 0 to at t = 1 when AB is at (x, 0)
{tex}\therefore \varepsilon_{2}=\frac{-d \phi}{d t}=\frac{-d}{d t} \vec{B} \cdot \vec{A}{/tex}{tex}=\frac{-d}{d t}\left[B_{0} \sin \omega t \hat{k} \cdot(x \cdot d) \hat{k}\right]{/tex}
Area vector A is along {tex}\hat{k}​​​{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \varepsilon_{2}=\frac{-d}{d t}\left(B_{0} \sin \omega t x d\right){/tex}
{tex}\varepsilon_{2}=-B_{0} x d . \omega \cos \omega{/tex} ...(ii)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Total magnitude of e.m.f. {tex}=-B_{0} v d \sin \omega t-B_{0} x d \omega \cos \omega t{/tex}
{tex}\varepsilon=-B_{0} d[v \sin \omega t+x \omega \cos \omega t]{/tex}
The direction of electric induced current by Fleming Right Hand Rule is from (A to B) i.e., in clockwise in the loop.
{tex}I=\frac{\varepsilon}{R}=\frac{B_{0} d}{R}{/tex} {tex}[\mathrm{v} \sin \omega \mathrm{t}+\omega \mathrm{x} \cos \omega \mathrm{t}]{/tex}
{tex}\theta{/tex} is the angle between {tex}B \hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\mathrm{I}(-\mathrm{j})=90^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{F}=\mathrm{I} \times \mathrm{Bdl}{/tex}
{tex}=I(-\hat{j}) \times B(\hat{k}) d \sin \theta\left[\because \theta=90^{\circ}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=-I B(\hat{j} \times \hat{k}) d \sin 90^{\circ}=-I B(\hat{i}) d{/tex}
{tex}F=-\hat{i} I B d{/tex}
It shows the direction of the force on AB is toward x-direction, which can be certified by Fleming's left-hand rule.
{tex}\therefore|F|=I B d{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} To keep the wire in motion with constant velocity, it will be towards positive x-direction + opp. To electromagnetic force
{tex}F=B \cdot d I=|B| \cdot d \frac{\varepsilon}{R}{/tex}
{tex}F=\frac{B d B_{0} d}{R}[v \sin \omega t+\omega x \cos \omega t]{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{B_{0} \sin \omega t B_{0} d^{2}}{R}[v \sin \omega t+\omega x \cos \omega t]{/tex}
{tex}F=\frac{B_{0}^{2} d^{2} \sin \omega t}{R}[v \sin \omega t+\omega x \cos \omega t]{/tex}

question_text: A conducting wire XY of mass m and negligible resistance slides smoothly on two parallel conducting wires as shown in Figure. The closed-circuit has a resistance R due to AC. AB and CD are perfect conductors. There is a magnetic field {tex}\mathbf{B}=B(t) \hat{\mathbf{k}}{/tex} . Write down the equation for the acceleration of the wire XY. If B is independent of time, obtain v(t), assuming v(O) = u 0 . For (b), show that the decrease in kinetic energy of XY equals the heat lost in R.
question_html:
A conducting wire XY of mass m and negligible resistance slides smoothly on two parallel conducting wires as shown in Figure. The closed-circuit has a resistance R due to AC. AB and CD are perfect conductors. There is a magnetic field {tex}\mathbf{B}=B(t) \hat{\mathbf{k}}{/tex}.

  1. Write down the equation for the acceleration of the wire XY.
  2. If B is independent of time, obtain v(t), assuming v(O) = u0.
  3. For (b), show that the decrease in kinetic energy of XY equals the heat lost in R.
solution_text: Let wire XY at t = 0 is at x = 0 And at t = t is at x = x(t) Magnetic flux is a function of time {tex}\phi{/tex} (t) = B(t) {tex}\times{/tex} A {tex}\therefore \phi(t)=B(t)[1 . x(t)]{/tex} {tex}\varepsilon=-\frac{d \phi(t)}{d t}=-\frac{d B(t)}{d t} l x(t)-B(t) l \cdot \frac{d x(t)}{d t}{/tex} {tex}\varepsilon=\frac{-d B(t)}{d t} l . x(t)-B(t) l v(t){/tex} The direction of induced current by Fleming's Right Hand Rule or by Lenz's law is in clockwise direction in loop XY < AX. {tex}I=\frac{\varepsilon}{R}=\frac{-l}{R}\left[x(t) \frac{d B(t)}{d t}+B(t) v(t)\right]{/tex} .... (i) The force acting on the conductor is F = B(t) Il sin 90 o F = B(t) I.l {tex}F=\frac{B(t) l \varepsilon}{R}=\frac{-B(t) l^{2}}{R}{/tex} {tex}\left[\frac{-d B(t)}{d t} x(t)-B(t) \cdot v(t)\right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{m d^{2} x}{d t^{2}}=\frac{-B(t) l^{2}}{R}\left[x(t) \frac{d B(t)}{d t}+B(t) v(t)\right]{/tex} Or {tex}\frac{d^{2} x}{d t^{2}}=\frac{-l^{2}}{m R} B(t)\left[\lambda(t) \frac{d B(t)}{d t}+B(t) \cdot u(t)\right]{/tex} ...(ii) Now B is independent of time i.e. B does not change with time or it is constant {tex}\therefore \frac{d B}{d t}=0{/tex} , B(t) = B and v (t) = v ...(iii) Put (iii) in (ii) we get {tex}\frac{d^{2} x}{d t^{2}}=\frac{-l^{2}}{m R}[0+B v]{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} x}{d t^{2}}+\frac{B^{2} l^{2}}{m R} \frac{d x}{d t}=0{/tex} {tex}\frac{d v}{d t}+\frac{B^{2} l^{2}}{m R} v=0{/tex} Integrating using variable separable from differential equation we have {tex}v=A \exp \left(\frac{-l^{2} B^{2} t}{m R}\right){/tex} at t = 0, v = u {tex}\therefore v(t)=u \exp \left(\frac{-l^{2} B^{2} t}{m R}\right){/tex} ...(iv) Similarly, for time interval {tex}\frac{3 \pi}{4 \omega}<t<\frac{\pi}{\omega} \text { or } \frac{3 T}{8}<t<\frac{T}{2}{/tex} , the rod will be in touch with AC. The flux through OQABD is given by {tex}\phi_{m}=\left(2 l^{2}-\frac{l^{2}}{2 \tan \omega t}\right) B{/tex} And the magnitude of emf generated in loop is given by {tex}\varepsilon=\frac{d \phi}{d t}=\frac{B \omega l^{2} \sec ^{2} \omega t}{2 \tan ^{2} \omega t}{/tex} These are the required expressions.
solution_html:
1. Let wire XY at t = 0 is at x = 0
    And at t = t is at x = x(t)
    Magnetic flux is a function of time {tex}\phi{/tex}(t) = B(t) {tex}\times{/tex} A
    {tex}\therefore \phi(t)=B(t)[1 . x(t)]{/tex}
    {tex}\varepsilon=-\frac{d \phi(t)}{d t}=-\frac{d B(t)}{d t} l x(t)-B(t) l \cdot \frac{d x(t)}{d t}{/tex}
    {tex}\varepsilon=\frac{-d B(t)}{d t} l . x(t)-B(t) l v(t){/tex}
    The direction of induced current by Fleming's Right Hand Rule or by Lenz's law is in clockwise direction in loop XY < AX.
    {tex}I=\frac{\varepsilon}{R}=\frac{-l}{R}\left[x(t) \frac{d B(t)}{d t}+B(t) v(t)\right]{/tex} .... (i)
    The force acting on the conductor is F = B(t) Il sin 90o
    F = B(t) I.l
    {tex}F=\frac{B(t) l \varepsilon}{R}=\frac{-B(t) l^{2}}{R}{/tex}{tex}\left[\frac{-d B(t)}{d t} x(t)-B(t) \cdot v(t)\right]{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{m d^{2} x}{d t^{2}}=\frac{-B(t) l^{2}}{R}\left[x(t) \frac{d B(t)}{d t}+B(t) v(t)\right]{/tex}
    Or {tex}\frac{d^{2} x}{d t^{2}}=\frac{-l^{2}}{m R} B(t)\left[\lambda(t) \frac{d B(t)}{d t}+B(t) \cdot u(t)\right]{/tex} ...(ii)
  2. Now B is independent of time i.e. B does not change with time or it is constant
    {tex}\therefore \frac{d B}{d t}=0{/tex}, B(t) = B and v (t) = v ...(iii)
    Put (iii) in (ii) we get
    {tex}\frac{d^{2} x}{d t^{2}}=\frac{-l^{2}}{m R}[0+B v]{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{d^{2} x}{d t^{2}}+\frac{B^{2} l^{2}}{m R} \frac{d x}{d t}=0{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{d v}{d t}+\frac{B^{2} l^{2}}{m R} v=0{/tex}
    Integrating using variable separable from differential equation we have
    {tex}v=A \exp \left(\frac{-l^{2} B^{2} t}{m R}\right){/tex}
    at t = 0, v  = u
    {tex}\therefore v(t)=u \exp \left(\frac{-l^{2} B^{2} t}{m R}\right){/tex} ...(iv)
  3. Similarly, for time interval {tex}\frac{3 \pi}{4 \omega}<t<\frac{\pi}{\omega} \text { or } \frac{3 T}{8}<t<\frac{T}{2}{/tex}, the rod will be in touch with AC.

    The flux through OQABD is given by
    {tex}\phi_{m}=\left(2 l^{2}-\frac{l^{2}}{2 \tan \omega t}\right) B{/tex}
    And the magnitude of emf generated in loop is given by
    {tex}\varepsilon=\frac{d \phi}{d t}=\frac{B \omega l^{2} \sec ^{2} \omega t}{2 \tan ^{2} \omega t}{/tex}
    These are the required expressions.

question_text: ODBAC is a fixed rectangular conductor of negligible resistance (CO is not connected) and OP is a conductor which rotates clockwise with an angular velocity {tex}\omega{/tex} (Fig). The entire system is in a uniform magnetic field B whose direction is along the normal to the surface of the rectangular conductor ABDC. The conductor OP is in electric contact with ABDC. The rotating conductor has a resistance of {tex}\lambda{/tex} per unit length. Find the current in the rotating conductor, as it rotates by 180°.
question_html: ODBAC is a fixed rectangular conductor of negligible resistance (CO is not connected) and OP is a conductor which rotates clockwise with an angular velocity {tex}\omega{/tex} (Fig). The entire system is in a uniform magnetic field B whose direction is along the normal to the surface of the rectangular conductor ABDC. The conductor OP is in electric contact with ABDC. The rotating conductor has a resistance of {tex}\lambda{/tex} per unit length. Find the current in the rotating conductor, as it rotates by 180°.
solution_text: Let us first assume the position of rotating conductor at a time interval t = 0 to {tex}t=\frac{\pi}{4 \omega}{/tex} (or T/8) in this question, ODBAC is a fixed rectangular conductor of negligible resistance (CO is not connected) and OP is a conductor which rotates clockwise with an angular velocity The rod OP will make contact with the side BD. Let the length OQ of the contact after some time interval 't' such that {tex}0<t<\frac{\pi}{4 \omega}{/tex} or {tex}0<t<\frac{T}{8}{/tex} be x. The flux through the area ODQ is {tex}\phi_{m}=B A=B\left(\frac{1}{2} \times Q D \times O D\right){/tex} {tex}=B\left(\frac{1}{2} \times l \tan \theta \times l\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \phi_{m}=\frac{1}{2} B l^{2} \tan \theta{/tex} , where {tex}\theta=\omega t{/tex} By applying Faraday's law of EMI, Thus, the magnitude of the emf induced is {tex}|\varepsilon|=\left|\frac{d \phi}{d t}\right|=\frac{1}{2} B l^{2} \omega \sec ^{2} \omega t{/tex} The current induced in the circuit will be {tex}I=\frac{\varepsilon}{R}{/tex} where, R is the resistance of the rod in contact. where, {tex}R \propto \lambda{/tex} {tex}R=\lambda x=\frac{\lambda l}{\cos \omega t}{/tex} {tex}\therefore I = \frac {1}{2} \frac {Bl^2 \omega}{\lambda l} sec^2 \omega t =\frac{B l \omega}{2 \lambda \cos \omega t}{/tex} Now let the rod OP will make contact with the side AB. And the length of OQ of the contact after some time interval t such that {tex}\frac{\pi}{4 \omega}<t<\frac{3 \pi}{4 \omega}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{T}{8}<t<\frac{3 T}{8}{/tex} be x. The flux through the area OQBD is {tex}\phi_{m}=\left(l^{2}+\frac{1}{2} \frac{l^{2}}{\tan \theta}\right) B{/tex} where, {tex}\theta=\omega t{/tex} Thus, the magnitude of emf induced in the loop is {tex}|\varepsilon|=\left|\frac{d \phi}{d t}\right|=\frac{B l^{2} \omega \sec ^{2} \omega t}{2 \tan ^{2} \omega t}{/tex} The current induced in the circuits is I {tex}=\frac{\varepsilon}{R}=\frac{\varepsilon}{\lambda x}=\frac{\varepsilon \sin \omega t}{\lambda l}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{B l \omega}{\lambda \sin \omega t}{/tex} Similarly, for time interval {tex}\frac{3 \pi}{4 \omega}<t<\frac{\pi}{\omega} \text { or } \frac{3 T}{8}<t<\frac{T}{2}{/tex} , the rod will be in touch with AC. The flux through OQABD is given by {tex}\phi_{m}=\left(2 l^{2}-\frac{l^{2}}{2 \tan \omega t}\right) B{/tex} And the magnitude of emf generated in the loop is given by {tex}\varepsilon=\frac{d \phi}{d t}=\frac{B \omega l^{2} \sec ^{2} \omega t}{2 \tan ^{2} \omega t}{/tex} {tex}I=\frac{\varepsilon}{R}=\frac{\varepsilon}{\lambda x}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{B l \omega}{\lambda \sin \omega t}{/tex} These are the required expressions.
solution_html:
1. Let us first assume the position of rotating conductor at a time interval
    t = 0 to {tex}t=\frac{\pi}{4 \omega}{/tex} (or T/8) in this question, ODBAC is a fixed rectangular conductor of negligible resistance (CO is not connected) and OP is a conductor which rotates clockwise with an angular velocity 

    The rod OP will make contact with the side BD. Let the length OQ of the contact after some time interval 't' such that {tex}0<t<\frac{\pi}{4 \omega}{/tex} or {tex}0<t<\frac{T}{8}{/tex} be x. The flux through the area ODQ is
    {tex}\phi_{m}=B A=B\left(\frac{1}{2} \times Q D \times O D\right){/tex} {tex}=B\left(\frac{1}{2} \times l \tan \theta \times l\right){/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \phi_{m}=\frac{1}{2} B l^{2} \tan \theta{/tex}, where {tex}\theta=\omega t{/tex}
    By applying Faraday's law of EMI,
    Thus, the magnitude of the emf induced is {tex}|\varepsilon|=\left|\frac{d \phi}{d t}\right|=\frac{1}{2} B l^{2} \omega \sec ^{2} \omega t{/tex}
    The current induced in the circuit will be {tex}I=\frac{\varepsilon}{R}{/tex} where, R is the resistance of the rod in contact.
    where, {tex}R \propto \lambda{/tex}
    {tex}R=\lambda x=\frac{\lambda l}{\cos \omega t}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore I = \frac {1}{2} \frac {Bl^2 \omega}{\lambda l} sec^2 \omega t =\frac{B l \omega}{2 \lambda \cos \omega t}{/tex}
  2. Now let the rod OP will make contact with the side AB. And the length of OQ of the contact after some time interval t such that {tex}\frac{\pi}{4 \omega}<t<\frac{3 \pi}{4 \omega}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{T}{8}<t<\frac{3 T}{8}{/tex} be x. The flux through the area OQBD is
    {tex}\phi_{m}=\left(l^{2}+\frac{1}{2} \frac{l^{2}}{\tan \theta}\right) B{/tex}
    where, {tex}\theta=\omega t{/tex}
    Thus, the magnitude of emf induced in the loop is
    {tex}|\varepsilon|=\left|\frac{d \phi}{d t}\right|=\frac{B l^{2} \omega \sec ^{2} \omega t}{2 \tan ^{2} \omega t}{/tex}
    The current induced in the circuits is I {tex}=\frac{\varepsilon}{R}=\frac{\varepsilon}{\lambda x}=\frac{\varepsilon \sin \omega t}{\lambda l}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{B l \omega}{\lambda \sin \omega t}{/tex}
  3. Similarly, for time interval {tex}\frac{3 \pi}{4 \omega}<t<\frac{\pi}{\omega} \text { or } \frac{3 T}{8}<t<\frac{T}{2}{/tex}, the rod will be in touch with AC.

    The flux through OQABD is given by
    {tex}\phi_{m}=\left(2 l^{2}-\frac{l^{2}}{2 \tan \omega t}\right) B{/tex}
    And the magnitude of emf generated in the loop is given by
    {tex}\varepsilon=\frac{d \phi}{d t}=\frac{B \omega l^{2} \sec ^{2} \omega t}{2 \tan ^{2} \omega t}{/tex}
    {tex}I=\frac{\varepsilon}{R}=\frac{\varepsilon}{\lambda x}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{B l \omega}{\lambda \sin \omega t}{/tex}
    These are the required expressions.

question_text: Consider an infinitely long wire carrying a current I (t ), with {tex}\frac{d I}{d t}=\lambda{/tex} = constant. Find the current produced in the rectangular loop of wire ABCD if its resistance is R (Figure).
question_html: Consider an infinitely long wire carrying a current I (t ), with {tex}\frac{d I}{d t}=\lambda{/tex} = constant. Find the current produced in the rectangular loop of wire ABCD if its resistance is R (Figure).
solution_text: Consider a strip of width dr and length l inside a rectangle at distance r from the surface of the current-carrying conductor. The magnetic field across the strip of length {tex}l=B(r)=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi r} l . B(r){/tex} is perpendicular to the paper upward. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Flux in strip {tex}\phi=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi} l \int_{x_{0}}^{x} \frac{d r}{r}{/tex} {tex}\phi=\frac{\mu_{0} I l}{2 \pi}\left[\log _{e} r\right]_{x_{0}}^{x}=\frac{\mu_{0} Il}{2 \pi} \log _{e} \frac{x}{x_{0}}{/tex} {tex}\varepsilon=\frac{-d \phi}{d t}{/tex} So {tex}I R=\frac{d \phi}{d t}{/tex} {tex}I=\frac{1}{R} \frac{d}{d t}\left[\frac{\mu_{0} Il}{2 \pi} \log _{\epsilon} \frac{x}{x_{0}}\right]=\frac{\mu_{0} l}{2 \pi R} \cdot \log _{\varepsilon} \frac{x}{x_{0}} \frac{d I}{d t}{/tex} {tex}I=\frac{\mu_{0} \lambda l}{2 \pi R} \log _{6} \frac{x}{x_{0}}\left[\because \frac{d I}{d t}=\lambda(\text {given})\right]{/tex}
solution_html:
Consider a strip of width dr and length l inside a rectangle at distance r from the surface of the current-carrying conductor. The magnetic field across the strip of length {tex}l=B(r)=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi r} l . B(r){/tex} is perpendicular to the paper upward.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Flux in strip {tex}\phi=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi} l \int_{x_{0}}^{x} \frac{d r}{r}{/tex}
{tex}\phi=\frac{\mu_{0} I l}{2 \pi}\left[\log _{e} r\right]_{x_{0}}^{x}=\frac{\mu_{0} Il}{2 \pi} \log _{e} \frac{x}{x_{0}}{/tex}
{tex}\varepsilon=\frac{-d \phi}{d t}{/tex}
So {tex}I R=\frac{d \phi}{d t}{/tex}
{tex}I=\frac{1}{R} \frac{d}{d t}\left[\frac{\mu_{0} Il}{2 \pi} \log _{\epsilon} \frac{x}{x_{0}}\right]=\frac{\mu_{0} l}{2 \pi R} \cdot \log _{\varepsilon} \frac{x}{x_{0}} \frac{d I}{d t}{/tex}
{tex}I=\frac{\mu_{0} \lambda l}{2 \pi R} \log _{6} \frac{x}{x_{0}}\left[\because \frac{d I}{d t}=\lambda(\text {given})\right]{/tex}

question_text: A rectangular loop of wire ABCD is kept close to an infinitely long wire carrying a current I(t) = l o (1 - {tex}\frac tT{/tex} ) for {tex}0 \leq t \leq T{/tex} and I(0) = 0 for t > T (Figure). Find the total charge passing through a given point in the loop, in time T. The resistance of the loop is R.
question_html: A rectangular loop of wire ABCD is kept close to an infinitely long wire carrying a current I(t) = lo (1 - {tex}\frac tT{/tex}) for {tex}0 \leq t \leq T{/tex} and I(0) = 0 for t > T (Figure). Find the total charge passing through a given point in the loop, in time T. The resistance of the loop is R.
solution_text: To find the charge that passes through the circuit first we have to find the relation between instantaneous current and instantaneous magnetic flux linked with it. The emf induced can be obtained by differentiating the expression of magnetic flux linked w.r.t. t and then applying Ohm's law, we get A rectangular loop of wire ABCD is kept close to an infinitely long wire carrying a current {tex}I=\frac{E}{R}=\frac{1}{R} \frac{d \phi}{d t}{/tex} According to the problem electric current is given as a function of time. {tex}I(t)=\frac{d Q}{d t} \text { or } \frac{d Q}{d t}=\frac{1}{R} \frac{d \phi}{d t}{/tex} Integrating the variable separately in the form of the differential equation for finding the charge Q that passed in time t, we have {tex}Q\left(t_{1}\right)-Q\left(t_{2}\right)=\frac{1}{R}\left[\phi\left(t_{1}\right)-\phi\left(t_{2}\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}\phi\left(t_{1}\right)=L_{1} \frac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \int \limits_{x}^{L_{2}+x} \frac{d x^{\prime}}{x^{\prime}} I\left(t_{1}\right){/tex} [ {tex}\phi_{m}=\vec{B} \cdot \vec{A}=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi} l \int \limits_{x_{0}}^{x} \frac{d r}{r}=\frac{\mu_{0} I l}{2 \pi} \ln \frac{x}{x_{0}}{/tex} ] {tex}=\frac{\mu_{0} L_{1}}{2 \pi} I\left(t_{1}\right) \ln \frac{L_{2}+x}{x}{/tex} Therefore the magnitude of charge is {tex}Q=\frac{1}{R}[\phi(T)-\phi(0)]{/tex} {tex}Q=\frac{\mu_{0} L_{1}}{2 \pi} \ln \frac{L_{2}+x}{x}[I(T)-I(0)]{/tex} Now I(T) = I 1 and I(0) = 0 {tex}\therefore \quad Q=\frac{\mu_{0} L_{1}}{2 \pi} I_{1} \ln \left(\frac{L_{2}+x}{x}\right){/tex} This is the required expression.
solution_html:
To find the charge that passes through the circuit first we have to find the relation between instantaneous current and instantaneous magnetic flux linked with it. The emf induced can be obtained by differentiating the expression of magnetic flux linked w.r.t. t and then applying Ohm's law, we get A rectangular loop of wire ABCD is kept close to an infinitely long wire carrying a current 
{tex}I=\frac{E}{R}=\frac{1}{R} \frac{d \phi}{d t}{/tex}
According to the problem electric current is given as a function of time.
{tex}I(t)=\frac{d Q}{d t} \text { or } \frac{d Q}{d t}=\frac{1}{R} \frac{d \phi}{d t}{/tex}
Integrating the variable separately in the form of the differential equation for finding the charge Q that passed in time t, we have
{tex}Q\left(t_{1}\right)-Q\left(t_{2}\right)=\frac{1}{R}\left[\phi\left(t_{1}\right)-\phi\left(t_{2}\right)\right]{/tex}
{tex}\phi\left(t_{1}\right)=L_{1} \frac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \int \limits_{x}^{L_{2}+x} \frac{d x^{\prime}}{x^{\prime}} I\left(t_{1}\right){/tex} [{tex}\phi_{m}=\vec{B} \cdot \vec{A}=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi} l \int \limits_{x_{0}}^{x} \frac{d r}{r}=\frac{\mu_{0} I l}{2 \pi} \ln \frac{x}{x_{0}}{/tex}]
{tex}=\frac{\mu_{0} L_{1}}{2 \pi} I\left(t_{1}\right) \ln \frac{L_{2}+x}{x}{/tex}
Therefore the magnitude of charge is
{tex}Q=\frac{1}{R}[\phi(T)-\phi(0)]{/tex}
{tex}Q=\frac{\mu_{0} L_{1}}{2 \pi} \ln \frac{L_{2}+x}{x}[I(T)-I(0)]{/tex}
Now I(T) = I1 and I(0) = 0
{tex}\therefore \quad Q=\frac{\mu_{0} L_{1}}{2 \pi} I_{1} \ln \left(\frac{L_{2}+x}{x}\right){/tex}
This is the required expression.

question_text: A magnetic field B is confined to a region r {tex}\leq{/tex} a and points out of the paper (the z-axis), r = 0 being the centre of the circular region. A charged ring (charge = Q) of radius b, b > a and mass m lies in the x-y plane with its centre at the origin. The ring is free to rotate and is at rest. The magnetic field is brought to zero in time {tex}\Delta t{/tex} . Find the angular velocity ω of the ring after the field vanishes.
question_html: A magnetic field B is confined to a region r {tex}\leq{/tex} a and points out of the paper (the z-axis), r = 0 being the centre of the circular region. A charged ring (charge = Q) of radius b, b > a and mass m lies in the x-y plane with its centre at the origin. The ring is free to rotate and is at rest. The magnetic field is brought to zero in time {tex}\Delta t{/tex}. Find the angular velocity ω of the ring after the field vanishes.
solution_text: By the relation between electric field and potential we get, The induced emf = Electric field E {tex}\times{/tex} (2 {tex}\pi b{/tex} ) (Because V = E {tex}\times{/tex} d) ..... (i) If E is the electric field generated around the charged ring of radius b, then as e By Faraday's law: {tex}|\varepsilon|=\frac{d \phi}{d t}=A \frac{d B}{d t}{/tex} {tex}|\varepsilon|=\frac{B \pi a^{2}}{\Delta t} {/tex} ...... (ii) From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we have {tex}2 \pi b E=\varepsilon=\frac{B \pi a^{2}}{\Delta t}{/tex} As we know the electric force experienced by the charged ring, F e = QE his force try to rotate the coil, and the torque is given by Torque = b {tex}\times{/tex} Force {tex}\tau=Q E b=Q\left[\frac{B \pi a^{2}}{2 \pi b \Delta t}\right] b{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \tau=Q \frac{B a^{2}}{2 \Delta t}{/tex} If {tex}\Delta L{/tex} is the change in angular momentum of the charged ring then, {tex}\Delta L=\text { Torque } \times \Delta t=Q \frac{B a^{2}}{2}{/tex} since, initial angular momentum = 0 And Torque {tex}\times \Delta t{/tex} = Change in angular momentum Final angular momentum {tex}=m b^{2} \omega=\frac{Q B a^{2}}{2}{/tex} Where, mb 2 = I (moment of inertia of ring) {tex}\omega=\frac{Q B a^{2}}{2 m b^{2}}{/tex} On rearranging the term, we have the required expression of angular speed.
solution_html:
By the relation between  electric field and potential we get,
The induced emf = Electric field E {tex}\times{/tex} (2{tex}\pi b{/tex}) (Because V = E {tex}\times{/tex} d) ..... (i)
If E is the electric field generated around the charged ring of radius b, then as e By Faraday's law:
{tex}|\varepsilon|=\frac{d \phi}{d t}=A \frac{d B}{d t}{/tex}
{tex}|\varepsilon|=\frac{B \pi a^{2}}{\Delta t} {/tex}...... (ii)
From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we have

As we know the electric force experienced by the charged ring, Fe = QE

his force try to rotate the coil, and the torque is given by
Torque = b {tex}\times{/tex} Force
{tex}\tau=Q E b=Q\left[\frac{B \pi a^{2}}{2 \pi b \Delta t}\right] b{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \tau=Q \frac{B a^{2}}{2 \Delta t}{/tex}
If {tex}\Delta L{/tex} is the change in angular momentum of the charged ring then,
{tex}\Delta L=\text { Torque } \times \Delta t=Q \frac{B a^{2}}{2}{/tex}

question_text: A rod of mass m and resistance R slides smoothly over two parallel perfectly conducting wires kept sloping at an angle θ with respect to the horizontal (Figure). The circuit is closed through a perfect conductor at the top. There is a constant magnetic field B along the vertical direction. If the rod is initially at rest, find the velocity of the rod as a function of time.
question_html: A rod of mass m and resistance R slides smoothly over two parallel perfectly conducting wires kept sloping at an angle θ with respect to the horizontal (Figure). The circuit is closed through a perfect conductor at the top. There is a constant magnetic field B along the vertical direction. If the rod is initially at rest, find the velocity of the rod as a function of time.
solution_text: Let us first divide the magnetic field in the components one is along the inclined plane = B sin {tex}\theta{/tex} and other component of magnetic field is perpendicular the plane = B cos {tex}\theta{/tex} Now, the conductor moves with speed v perpendicular to B cos {tex}\theta{/tex} component of magnetic field. This causes motional emf across two ends of rod, which is given by = v(B cos {tex}\theta{/tex} )d This makes flow of induced current i {tex}=\frac{v(B \cos \theta) d}{R}{/tex} where R is the resistance of rod. Now current-carrying rod experience a magnetic force which is given by F m = iBd (horizontally in backward direction). Now, the component of magnetic force parallel to the inclined plane along upward direction {tex}F_{\|}=F_{\mathrm{m}} \cos \theta=i B d \cos \theta{/tex} {tex}=\left(\frac{v(B \cos \theta) d}{R}\right) B d \cos \theta{/tex} where, {tex}v=\frac{d x}{d t}{/tex} Also, the component of weight (mg) parallel to the inclined plane along downward direction = mg sin {tex}\theta{/tex} Now, by Newton's second law of motion {tex}m \frac{d^{2} x}{d t^{2}}=m g \sin \theta{/tex} {tex}-\frac{B \cos \theta d}{R}\left(\frac{d x}{d t}\right) \times(B d) \cos \theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d v}{d t}=g \sin \theta-\frac{B^{2} d^{2}}{m R}(\cos \theta)^{2} v{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d v}{d t}+\frac{B^{2} d^{2}}{m R}(\cos \theta)^{2} v=g \sin \theta{/tex} But, this is the linear differential equation. On solving, we get {tex}v=\frac{g \sin \theta}{\frac{B^{2} d^{2} \cos ^{2} \theta}{m R}}+A \exp \left(-\frac{B^{2} d^{2}}{m R}\left(\cos ^{2} \theta\right) t\right){/tex} A is a constant to be determined by initial conditions. The required expression of velocity as a function of time is given by {tex}=\frac{m g R \sin \theta}{B^{2} d^{2} \cos ^{2} \theta}\left(1-\exp \left(-\frac{B^{2} d^{2}}{m R}\left(\cos ^{2} \theta\right) t\right)\right){/tex}
solution_html:
Let us first divide the magnetic field in the components one is along the inclined plane = B sin {tex}\theta{/tex} and other component of magnetic field is perpendicular the plane = B cos {tex}\theta{/tex}
Now, the conductor moves with speed v perpendicular to B cos {tex}\theta{/tex} component of magnetic field. This causes motional emf across two ends of rod, which is
given by = v(B cos {tex}\theta{/tex})d

This makes flow of induced current i {tex}=\frac{v(B \cos \theta) d}{R}{/tex} where R is the resistance of rod. Now current-carrying rod experience a magnetic force which is given by Fm = iBd (horizontally in backward direction). Now, the component of magnetic force parallel to the inclined plane along upward direction
{tex}F_{\|}=F_{\mathrm{m}} \cos \theta=i B d \cos \theta{/tex} {tex}=\left(\frac{v(B \cos \theta) d}{R}\right) B d \cos \theta{/tex}
where, {tex}v=\frac{d x}{d t}{/tex}
Also, the component of weight (mg) parallel to the inclined plane along downward direction = mg sin {tex}\theta{/tex}
Now, by Newton's second law of motion
{tex}m \frac{d^{2} x}{d t^{2}}=m g \sin \theta{/tex} {tex}-\frac{B \cos \theta d}{R}\left(\frac{d x}{d t}\right) \times(B d) \cos \theta{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d v}{d t}=g \sin \theta-\frac{B^{2} d^{2}}{m R}(\cos \theta)^{2} v{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d v}{d t}+\frac{B^{2} d^{2}}{m R}(\cos \theta)^{2} v=g \sin \theta{/tex}
But, this is the linear differential equation.
On solving, we get
{tex}v=\frac{g \sin \theta}{\frac{B^{2} d^{2} \cos ^{2} \theta}{m R}}+A \exp \left(-\frac{B^{2} d^{2}}{m R}\left(\cos ^{2} \theta\right) t\right){/tex}
A is a constant to be determined by initial conditions.
The required expression of velocity as a function of time is given by
{tex}=\frac{m g R \sin \theta}{B^{2} d^{2} \cos ^{2} \theta}\left(1-\exp \left(-\frac{B^{2} d^{2}}{m R}\left(\cos ^{2} \theta\right) t\right)\right){/tex}

question_text: Find the current in the sliding rod AB (resistance = R) for the arrangement shown in Figure. B is constant and is out of the paper. Parallel wires have no resistance. v is constant. Switch S is closed at time t = 0.
question_html: Find the current in the sliding rod AB (resistance = R) for the arrangement shown in Figure. B is constant and is out of the paper. Parallel wires have no resistance. v is constant. Switch S is closed at time t = 0.
solution_text: This is the similar problem as we discussed above. Here, a conductor of length d moves with speed v, perpendicular to the magnetic field B as shown in figure. Due to this a motional emf is induced across two ends of rod (e = vBd). since, switch S is closed at time t = 0, capacitor is charged by this potential difference. Let Q (t) be the charge on the capacitor and current flows from A to B. Now, the induced current {tex}I=\frac{d \mathcal{Q}}{d t}=\frac{B v d}{R}-\frac{\mathcal{Q}}{R C}{/tex} {tex}\frac{Q}{R C}+\frac{d Q}{d t}=\frac{B v d}{R}{/tex} {tex}Q+R C \frac{d Q}{d t}=v B C d{/tex} (Let vBdC = A).......(1) {tex}Q+R C \frac{dQ}{d t} =A{/tex} {tex}\frac{d Q}{A-Q}=\frac{1}{R C} d t{/tex} By integrating we have {tex}\int \limits_{0}^{Q} \frac{d Q}{A-Q}=\frac{1}{R C} \int \limits_{0}^{t} d t{/tex} {tex}= -[\ln (A-Q)-\ln A]=\frac{t}{R C}{/tex} {tex}\ln \frac{A-Q}{A}=-\frac{t}{R C}{/tex} {tex}\frac{A-Q}{A}=e^{-t / R C}{/tex} ( by taking exponential on both sides) Q = A(1 - e -t/RC ) Current in the rod is given by :- {tex}I=\frac{d Q}{d t}=\frac{d}{d t}\left[A\left(1-e^{-t / R C}\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}=-A\left(e^{-t / R C}\right)\left(-\frac{1}{R C}\right){/tex} thus from 1st equation {tex}I=\frac{v B d}{R} e^{-t / R C}{/tex}
solution_html:
This is the similar problem as we discussed above. Here, a conductor of length d moves with speed v, perpendicular to the magnetic field B as shown in figure. Due to this a motional emf is induced across two ends of rod (e = vBd). since, switch S is closed at time t = 0, capacitor is charged by this potential difference. Let Q (t) be the charge on the capacitor and current flows from A to B. Now, the induced current
{tex}I=\frac{d \mathcal{Q}}{d t}=\frac{B v d}{R}-\frac{\mathcal{Q}}{R C}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{Q}{R C}+\frac{d Q}{d t}=\frac{B v d}{R}{/tex}
{tex}Q+R C \frac{d Q}{d t}=v B C d{/tex} (Let vBdC = A).......(1)
{tex}Q+R C \frac{dQ}{d t} =A{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d Q}{A-Q}=\frac{1}{R C} d t{/tex}
By integrating we have
{tex}\int \limits_{0}^{Q} \frac{d Q}{A-Q}=\frac{1}{R C} \int \limits_{0}^{t} d t{/tex} {tex}= -[\ln (A-Q)-\ln A]=\frac{t}{R C}{/tex}
{tex}\ln \frac{A-Q}{A}=-\frac{t}{R C}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{A-Q}{A}=e^{-t / R C}{/tex}( by taking exponential on both sides)
Q = A(1 - e-t/RC)
Current in the rod is given by :-
{tex}I=\frac{d Q}{d t}=\frac{d}{d t}\left[A\left(1-e^{-t / R C}\right)\right]{/tex}
{tex}=-A\left(e^{-t / R C}\right)\left(-\frac{1}{R C}\right){/tex} thus from 1st equation
{tex}I=\frac{v B d}{R} e^{-t / R C}{/tex}

question_text: Find the current in the sliding rod AB (resistance = R) for the arrangement shown in Figure. B is constant and is out of the paper. Parallel wires have no resistance, v is constant. Switch S is closed at time t = 0.
question_html: Find the current in the sliding rod AB (resistance = R) for the arrangement shown in Figure. B is constant and is out of the paper. Parallel wires have no resistance, v is constant. Switch S is closed at time t = 0.
solution_text: Conductor AB moves towards right with speed v and magnetic {tex}\vec B{/tex} perpendicularly upward so angle between B and v is 90 o and an induced e.m.f., flows in loop AXYB. {tex}\varepsilon=\frac{d}{d t} \vec{B}. \vec{A}=\frac{d}{d t} B A \cos 0=\frac{d}{d t} B(d \times v \times t)=B v d{/tex} {tex}\because{/tex} angle between {tex}\vec B{/tex} and {tex}\vec A{/tex} is 0 o . {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = B.v.d at t= 0, current starts increasing in loop along with inductor L due to potential difference. By Kirchhoff’s law in loop ABYX, {tex}-L \frac{d I(t)}{d t}+B v d=RI(t){/tex} {tex}\frac{L d I(t)}{d t}+R I(t)=B v d{/tex} It is a differential equation. {tex}I(t)=\frac{B v d}{R}+A e^{-\frac{R t}{L}}{/tex} .....(i) at t = 0, l = 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}0=\frac{B v d}{R}+A e^{0}{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} e 0 = 1] {tex}A=\frac{-B v d}{R}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}I(t)=\frac{B v d}{R}-\frac{B v d}{R} e^{-\frac{R t}{L}}{/tex} [from (i)] {tex}I(t)=\frac{B v d}{R}\left[1-e^{-\frac{R t}{L}}\right]{/tex} This is the required current expression.
solution_html:
Conductor AB moves towards right with speed v and magnetic {tex}\vec B{/tex} perpendicularly upward so angle between B and v is 90o and an induced e.m.f., flows in loop AXYB.
{tex}\varepsilon=\frac{d}{d t} \vec{B}. \vec{A}=\frac{d}{d t} B A \cos 0=\frac{d}{d t} B(d \times v \times t)=B v d{/tex}
{tex}\because{/tex} angle between {tex}\vec B{/tex} and {tex}\vec A{/tex} is 0o.
{tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = B.v.d
at t= 0, current starts increasing in loop along with inductor L due to potential difference. By Kirchhoff’s law in loop ABYX,
{tex}-L \frac{d I(t)}{d t}+B v d=RI(t){/tex}
{tex}\frac{L d I(t)}{d t}+R I(t)=B v d{/tex}
It is a differential equation.
{tex}I(t)=\frac{B v d}{R}+A e^{-\frac{R t}{L}}{/tex}  .....(i)
at t = 0, l = 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}0=\frac{B v d}{R}+A e^{0}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} e0 = 1]
{tex}A=\frac{-B v d}{R}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}I(t)=\frac{B v d}{R}-\frac{B v d}{R} e^{-\frac{R t}{L}}{/tex} [from (i)]
{tex}I(t)=\frac{B v d}{R}\left[1-e^{-\frac{R t}{L}}\right]{/tex}
This is the required current expression.

question_text: A metallic ring of mass m and radius l (ring being horizontal) is falling under gravity in a region having a magnetic field. If z is the vertical direction, the z-component of magnetic field is B z = B o (1 + {tex}\lambda{/tex} z). If R is the resistance of the ring and if the ring falls with a velocity v, find the energy lost in the resistance. If the ring has reached a constant velocity, use the conservation of energy to determine v in terms of m, B, {tex}\lambda{/tex} and acceleration due to gravity g.
question_html: A metallic ring of mass m and radius l (ring being horizontal) is falling under gravity in a region having a magnetic field. If z is the vertical direction, the z-component of magnetic field is Bz = Bo (1 + {tex}\lambda{/tex}z). If R is the resistance of the ring and if the ring falls with a velocity v, find the energy lost in the resistance. If the ring has reached a constant velocity, use the conservation of energy to determine v in terms of m, B, {tex}\lambda{/tex} and acceleration due to gravity g.
solution_text: Magnetic flux across the ring of mass ‘m’ and radius ‘l’ falling under gravity in a region of having magnetic field B z = B 0 (1 + {tex}\lambda{/tex} z) is {tex}\phi=\vec{B}_{z} \cdot \vec{A}=B_{0}(1+\lambda z) \cdot \pi l^{2}{/tex} The angle between {tex}\vec B{/tex} and {tex}\vec A{/tex} is 0 o . {tex}\varepsilon=\frac{d}{d t}\left[B_{0}(1+\lambda z)\right] \pi l^{2}{/tex} As, e = IR, thus, {tex}I=\frac{B_{0} \pi \lambda l^{2}}{R} \frac{d z}{d t}=\frac{B_{0} \pi \lambda l^{2}}{R} v{/tex} Energy lost = {tex}I^{2} R=\frac{B_{0}^{2} \pi^{2} \lambda^{2} l^{4}}{R^{2}} v^{2} R{/tex} = {tex}\frac{B_{0}^{2} \pi^{2} \lambda^{2} l^{4} v^{2}}{R}{/tex} The energy must come from decrease in P.E = {tex}m g \frac{d z}{d t}=m g v{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}m g v=\frac{B_{0}^{2} \pi^{2} \lambda^{2} v^{2} l^{4}}{R}{/tex} {tex}v=\frac{m g R}{B_{0}^{2} \pi^{2} \lambda^{2} l^{4}}{/tex} or {tex}v=\frac{m g R}{\left(\pi l^{2} \lambda B_{0}\right)^{2}}{/tex} It is the required relation.
solution_html:
Magnetic flux across the ring of mass ‘m’ and radius ‘l’ falling under gravity in a region of having magnetic field Bz = B0(1 + {tex}\lambda{/tex}z) is
{tex}\phi=\vec{B}_{z} \cdot \vec{A}=B_{0}(1+\lambda z) \cdot \pi l^{2}{/tex}
The angle between {tex}\vec B{/tex} and {tex}\vec A{/tex} is 0o.
{tex}\varepsilon=\frac{d}{d t}\left[B_{0}(1+\lambda z)\right] \pi l^{2}{/tex}
As, e = IR, thus,
{tex}I=\frac{B_{0} \pi \lambda l^{2}}{R} \frac{d z}{d t}=\frac{B_{0} \pi \lambda l^{2}}{R} v{/tex} 
Energy lost = {tex}I^{2} R=\frac{B_{0}^{2} \pi^{2} \lambda^{2} l^{4}}{R^{2}} v^{2} R{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{B_{0}^{2} \pi^{2} \lambda^{2} l^{4} v^{2}}{R}{/tex}
The energy must come from decrease in P.E = {tex}m g \frac{d z}{d t}=m g v{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}m g v=\frac{B_{0}^{2} \pi^{2} \lambda^{2} v^{2} l^{4}}{R}{/tex}
{tex}v=\frac{m g R}{B_{0}^{2} \pi^{2} \lambda^{2} l^{4}}{/tex} or {tex}v=\frac{m g R}{\left(\pi l^{2} \lambda B_{0}\right)^{2}}{/tex}
It is the required relation.

question_text: A long solenoid S has n turns per meter, with a diameter a. At the center of this coil we place a smaller coil of N turns and diameter b (where b < a). If the current in the solenoid increases linearly, with time, what is the induced emf appearing in the smaller coil. Plot graph showing the nature of variation in emf, if current varies as a function of mt 2 + C.
question_html: A long solenoid S has n turns per meter, with a diameter a. At the center of this coil we place a smaller coil of N turns and diameter b (where b < a). If the current in the solenoid increases linearly, with time, what is the induced emf appearing in the smaller coil. Plot graph showing the nature of variation in emf, if current varies as a function of mt2 + C.
solution_text: Varying magnetic field B(t) in solenoid is {tex}B_{1}(t)=\mu_{0} n I(t){/tex} This varying magnetic field changes flux in the smaller coil. Magnetic flux in IInd coil {tex}\phi_{2}=B_{1}(t) \cdot A{/tex} = {tex}\mu_{0} n I(t) \pi b^{2}{/tex} Induced e.m.f. in second coil due to solenoid’s varying magnetic field in 1 turn {tex}\varepsilon^{\prime}=\frac{-d \phi_{2}}{d t}=\frac{-d}{d t} \mu_{0} n \pi b^{2} I(t){/tex} = {tex}-\mu_{0} n \pi b^{2} \frac{d}{d t}\left(m t^{2}+C\right){/tex} = {tex}-\mu_{0} n \pi b^{2} .2 m t{/tex} So net e.m.f. produced in N turns of smaller coil {tex}\varepsilon=-\mu_{0} N n \pi b^{2} 2 m t{/tex} Negative sign signifies opposite nature of induced emf. The magnitude of emf varies with time as shown in the Fig.
solution_html:
Varying magnetic field B(t) in solenoid is
{tex}B_{1}(t)=\mu_{0} n I(t){/tex}
This varying magnetic field changes flux in the smaller coil.
Magnetic flux in IInd coil
{tex}\phi_{2}=B_{1}(t) \cdot A{/tex}
= {tex}\mu_{0} n I(t) \pi b^{2}{/tex}
Induced e.m.f. in second coil due to solenoid’s varying magnetic field in 1 turn
{tex}\varepsilon^{\prime}=\frac{-d \phi_{2}}{d t}=\frac{-d}{d t} \mu_{0} n \pi b^{2} I(t){/tex}
= {tex}-\mu_{0} n \pi b^{2} \frac{d}{d t}\left(m t^{2}+C\right){/tex}
= {tex}-\mu_{0} n \pi b^{2} .2 m t{/tex}
So net e.m.f. produced in N turns of smaller coil
{tex}\varepsilon=-\mu_{0} N n \pi b^{2} 2 m t{/tex}
Negative sign signifies opposite nature of induced emf.
The magnitude of emf varies with time as shown in the Fig.
